title: Travels With My Dad

jokes.text: If this phone rings... Let someone else answer it. / It doesn't matter, as long as it's not you.
jokes.setup: Louie gives Iggy what seems like an important responsibility
jokes.punchline: The 'responsibility' is specifically to NOT do anything
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - Louie's cruel manipulation of Iggy's eagerness to help. The misdirection is expertly crafted with clear setup and devastating punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Iggy, I'm giving you a very important responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Yeah? What is it, Louie?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I want you to do absolutely nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Nothing? That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's it. And don't screw it up.

jokes.text: You think you can handle that? / With my eyes closed.
jokes.setup: Louie asks if Iggy can handle not answering the phone
jokes.punchline: Iggy confidently declares he can do nothing with his eyes closed
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that extends the joke - Iggy's misplaced confidence in his ability to do absolutely nothing is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Can you handle not answering the phone?

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: I can do nothing with my eyes closed.

jokes.text: You're the kid from Lassie!
jokes.setup: Jim trying to place who Angie is
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong identification with a children's TV show reference
jokes.explanation: Simple mistaken identity joke that establishes Jim's character confusion. Economy is good but concept is familiar.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. Angie... Angie. Do I know you from somewhere?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Well, we've met a few times at the garage.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no, that's not it. Oh! You're that woman from Captain Kangaroo!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: What? No!

jokes.text: You were passed out on the floor.
jokes.setup: Angie explaining why Jim might not remember him
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of Jim being unconscious drunk
jokes.explanation: Mild embarrassment humor that reveals character backstory while getting a chuckle from the casual delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: He was unconscious drunk.

jokes.text: I'm working on a very important assignment for the boss.
jokes.setup: Jim declining coffee with Angie
jokes.punchline: Jim takes seriously his 'assignment' to not answer the phone
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Jim completely bought into Louie's manipulation. The irony works well given what we know.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No thanks, Angie. I'm on an assignment.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: What kind of assignment?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm not supposed to answer the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Who gave you that assignment?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My mother. She said, 'Don't answer the phone, it might be a bill collector.' So I'm not answering it.

jokes.text: Whew! I did it.
jokes.setup: Phone rings and stops, Jim celebrates
jokes.punchline: Jim is proud of successfully doing nothing
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - two words that perfectly capture Jim's misplaced pride in his 'accomplishment.' Great callback payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I did it! I finally did it!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I answered the phone before it rang.

jokes.text: I've been on a boat for three long, lonely months. / That's okay, I understand. / Oh, great. Uh, once.
jokes.setup: Angie explains his overly enthusiastic greeting to Elaine
jokes.punchline: Elaine's understanding permission followed by Angie's immediate plan to take advantage
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment with the awkward misunderstanding and Angie's inappropriate enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, well go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy, oh boy!

jokes.text: What would you have done? / I'd have screamed. / Uh-uh. It would've fallen in your mouth.
jokes.setup: Angie asks what Elaine would do about spider on face
jokes.punchline: Angie's practical but disgusting logic about screaming
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of expected response with gross-out logic that's both funny and practical. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'd scream.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: That's what I'm afraid of. You scream, the spider gets scared, he bites you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So what do I do?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You smile. The spider thinks you're friendly, he crawls off.

jokes.text: I don't know. I fell asleep. When I woke up, it was gone.
jokes.setup: Built up tension about what happened with the spider
jokes.punchline: Completely anticlimactic resolution - he just fell asleep
jokes.explanation: Excellent anticlimax that completely deflates the built-up tension. Perfect misdirection from adventure story to mundane reality.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: So what happened with the spider?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Well, I was sitting there, staring at it, getting more and more nervous...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: My heart was pounding, my palms were sweating, I couldn't move...

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: And then?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I fell asleep.

jokes.text: I don't think that would make a movie. / You don't? / No, maybe something for television.
jokes.setup: Angie suggested his story could be a movie
jokes.punchline: Alex's diplomatic but brutal assessment downgrading it to TV
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan put-down that's polite but devastating. The TV vs movie distinction is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A movie? Well, that's very kind of you to say, Angie. But let's be honest — it's more of a TV movie.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: What's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: About forty million dollars.

jokes.text: Gee, oh, I'm glad I didn't think of that.
jokes.setup: Alex suggests more spiders could have crawled on his face
jokes.punchline: Angie's innocent relief at not considering this terrifying possibility
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment - Angie's oblivious gratitude for not being given more nightmare fuel.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, there could have been more spiders crawling on my face.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I wasn't even thinking about that!

jokes.text: Pea Brain Senior.
jokes.setup: Louie sees Angie has arrived
jokes.punchline: His immediate insult connecting Angie to Tony's supposed stupidity
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult with good economy and character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey Angie, how's Tony doing? Still as dumb as ever?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: What's that supposed to mean?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I mean, you married him, didn't you? That says everything.

jokes.text: Back off, Banta. There's no tellin' what parasites you got crawling on you.
jokes.setup: Angie greets Louie
jokes.punchline: Louie's immediate hostile response about parasites
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - immediately insulting and specific to Angie's seafaring background. Great character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Hi, Louie!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Get away from me, you parasite! You're always hanging around here trying to mooch off me!

jokes.text: Did you fall overboard, or are you coming to get the barnacles scraped off your butt?
jokes.setup: Continuing Louie's hostility toward Angie
jokes.punchline: Escalated insult comparing Angie to a ship's hull
jokes.explanation: Creative maritime-themed insult that escalates from parasites to barnacles. Very quotable line.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what you are, Angie? You're like the hull of a ship—crusty, full of barnacles, and nobody wants to scrape you!

jokes.text: We'd trade him for that spider in a minute.
jokes.setup: Angie comments on Louie making the job fun
jokes.punchline: Alex would prefer the dangerous spider to Louie
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that connects the spider story to current situation. Great economy and perfectly expresses the drivers' feelings about Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You know, Louie really makes this job fun.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Fun? I'd take a dangerous spider over Louie any day.

jokes.text: I just came in to have the barnacles scraped off my butt. / Oh, no, Dad, what happened?
jokes.setup: Tony asks what brings his dad to town
jokes.punchline: Angie uses Louie's insult as his answer, Tony takes it literally
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Tony's innocent concern about his father's barnacle problem. Shows family dynamic well.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What brings my dad to town?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: He came to see what a bum you turned out to be.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: He came to see what a bum I turned out to be?

jokes.text: It's not funny. / You had to be here. / You'd have roared.
jokes.setup: Tony says the joke isn't funny
jokes.punchline: Angie's misguided confidence that Tony would have loved it if present
jokes.explanation: Mild irony - Angie completely misjudges what Tony would find funny. Shows his obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That joke isn't funny.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You would have loved it if you were here.

jokes.text: I don't know, but it's laminated.
jokes.setup: Tony looking at his seaman's permit
jokes.punchline: Tony's impressed by the lamination rather than the opportunity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony response - missing the big picture to focus on a trivial detail. Excellent economy with just five words.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, look at this! They laminated it! You see how nice and shiny it is? This is quality work. They don't make permits like this anymore.

jokes.text: It's like being in prison with a chance of drowning.
jokes.setup: Tony asks what it's like living on a ship
jokes.punchline: Angie's brutally honest description
jokes.explanation: Brilliant, quotable line that perfectly captures the misery of seafaring life. Original phrasing with excellent dark humor.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: It's like living in a closet with a man who smells like fish and thinks he's Columbus.

jokes.text: I've got to mark this on my calendar... 'The Day Banta was Dumbfounded.'
jokes.setup: Tony says he's dumbfounded by the sudden opportunity
jokes.punchline: Louie treats Tony being speechless as a historic event
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - turning Tony's rare moment of being speechless into an insult about his usual chattiness.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm dumbfounded by this sudden opportunity.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, speechless? This is a historic event. Someone call the newspapers!

jokes.text: Ever since I was five... till I was 11.
jokes.setup: Tony claiming he always wanted to be a merchant seaman
jokes.punchline: The very specific and brief childhood phase
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of the romantic notion with specific ages that show how fleeting the dream was. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I always wanted to be a merchant seaman.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: For about twenty minutes when I was eight.

jokes.text: I just learned how to use my Mr. Coffee.
jokes.setup: Tony listing reasons he can't leave
jokes.punchline: The absurdly mundane accomplishment of mastering a coffee maker
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Tony's attachment to small domestic victories. The specificity of 'Mr. Coffee' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I can't leave. I finally got this coffee maker working.

jokes.text: That's my father, Alex. You don't say no to your father.
jokes.setup: Alex advises Tony to say no
jokes.punchline: Tony's immediate rejection of perfectly reasonable advice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's inability to assert himself with his father despite good advice.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You should learn to say no, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Of course, on my first trip, I fed the fish from here to Fiji.
jokes.setup: Angie saying Tony will get used to seasickness quickly
jokes.punchline: Angie's own experience was much worse and longer lasting
jokes.explanation: Nice euphemism for vomiting with good geographic specificity. Undercuts his reassurance to Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Oh, he'll get used to it. Most people do pretty quick.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: When I was on a boat once, I was sick for three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Course, that was a different kind of boat.

jokes.text: Would you kill me?
jokes.setup: Angie asking Tony to let him know if he has misgivings
jokes.punchline: Tony's immediate, extreme request for death rather than honesty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony response - preferring death to disappointing his father. Excellent economy and character truth.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Let me know if you have any misgivings.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Kill me now.

jokes.text: It's also going to look a lot like the international date line you made me get up to see.
jokes.setup: Angie excited about crossing the equator
jokes.punchline: Tony's deflating reference to previous identical disappointment
jokes.explanation: Good running gag setup - Tony's growing awareness that oceanic landmarks all look like water.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: We're crossing the equator! Can you believe it? This is so exciting!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, just like when we crossed it last year.

jokes.text: What the hell are you looking at?
jokes.setup: Angie pointing out something in the water during 'equator crossing'
jokes.punchline: Tony's hostile reaction to what's probably just debris
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's growing irritation with the sea and his father's enthusiasm. Good character development moment.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Tony, look! There's something in the water!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What? Where? That better not be another one of your 'sightings.' Last time you made us stop for a floating coconut!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: No, seriously, I see something out there!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: It's debris, Angie. Probably a log or garbage. Not everything in the ocean is a sea monster or a message from the universe!

jokes.text: Some of those squid are 150 feet long. I've heard tell of them dragging a man down screaming into the sea.
jokes.setup: Tony dismissing giant squid stories
jokes.punchline: Angie's graphic and terrifying details
jokes.explanation: Classic father behavior - trying to scare son despite saying he can't be scared anymore. Good vivid imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Giant squid? Come on, there's no such thing as a giant squid.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: No such thing? Tony, I've seen them. I've seen their tentacles wrap around a ship like they're playing with a toy. Their eyes are the size of dinner plates, just staring at you with this cold, dead intelligence. And the suckers on those tentacles — they leave marks on the hull big enough to drive a truck through.

jokes.text: That's the way I'm starting to feel about women.
jokes.setup: Bill liking to believe giant squid exist
jokes.punchline: Tony applying the same logic to women
jokes.explanation: Excellent unexpected connection between mythical sea creatures and Tony's dating life. Perfect economy and character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So you believe in the giant squid because nobody's ever proven it doesn't exist?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Well, by that logic, I believe there's a woman out there who actually enjoys my company.

jokes.text: I also think about fresh produce. / Boy, what I wouldn't give for a nice, fresh, crispy head of lettuce right now.
jokes.setup: Angie explaining what he thinks about besides women
jokes.punchline: His specific, passionate craving for lettuce
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from women to vegetables, with specific craving that shows deprivation of sea life.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You know what I think about besides women? Lettuce. I'm serious! I dream about lettuce. I wake up at three in the morning thinking about crisp, cool, fresh lettuce. It's all I can think about sometimes.

jokes.text: That is, if you fall in love with her first.
jokes.setup: Angie suggesting Tony can sleep with women in port
jokes.punchline: The moral clarification that deflates the excitement
jokes.explanation: Good father figure moment - building up excitement then adding the moral caveat. Classic parental backpedaling.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You could sleep with women in every port.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Sure, as long as you're married to me.

jokes.text: Six hours.
jokes.setup: Tony asking how long they'll be in port
jokes.punchline: The impossibly short time frame for romance
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that makes falling in love even more absurd. Excellent economy with just two words.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: How long will we be in port?

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Three days.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Three days? That's not enough time!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Enough time for what?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: To fall in love with you and break your heart.

jokes.text: I might've even understood. / No kidding? / Hmm. / You're telling me.
jokes.setup: Tony saying he would understand if his father had been unfaithful
jokes.punchline: Angie's immediate regret about his fidelity
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about missed opportunities for infidelity. Good father-son bonding over inappropriate topics.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I would understand if my father had been unfaithful.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Oh, Tony... I wish you hadn't said that.

jokes.text: You're telling me.
jokes.setup: Tony says they should have had these talks long ago
jokes.punchline: Angie's rueful agreement implies regret about his marital fidelity
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character consistency, showing Angie's growing regret

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: We should have had these talks long ago.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we should have.

jokes.text: I don't even know what a sea chantey is. / It's a song about the sea. / 'New York, New York'? / Perfect.
jokes.setup: Tony explaining what a sea chantey is
jokes.punchline: Fergie's completely wrong but enthusiastically accepted suggestion
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist moment - 'New York, New York' as a sea chantey is perfectly wrong but gets immediate approval.

dialogue.speaker: Fergie
dialogue.line: A sea chantey? That's a song sailors sing while they're working on the ship, right?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's right.

dialogue.speaker: Fergie
dialogue.line: Oh! Like 'What shall we do with a drunken sailor'?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Fergie
dialogue.line: Man, those guys really knew how to party.

jokes.text: New York, New York? Perfect.
jokes.setup: Tony explains sea chanteys as songs about rum, women, and sailing
jokes.punchline: Fergie's suggestion of the quintessentially urban anthem as a sea song
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd connection that creates comedy through the contrast between maritime tradition and urban reality

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Sea chanteys are all about rum, women, and sailing.

dialogue.speaker: Fergie
dialogue.line: What about 'New York, New York'?

jokes.text: Perfect.
jokes.setup: Fergie suggests 'New York, New York' as a sea chantey
jokes.punchline: Tony's enthusiastic acceptance of the illogical choice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's desperation to connect with his father.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: New York, New York? That's perfect! That's a great sea chantey!

jokes.text: No, that comes later. What's the matter with you? / No, no, we did vagabond shoes.
jokes.setup: Father and son trying to sing together
jokes.punchline: Getting confused and arguing about the lyrics
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy with them getting tangled up in a song they both should know. Shows their relationship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Come on, let's sing together. I'll teach you the old songs.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Okay, Dad. Which one?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Santa Lucia. Ready? When the moon hits your eye like a big pizza pie, that's amore.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not Santa Lucia. That's 'That's Amore.'

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What do you mean? Of course it's Santa Lucia!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: No, it's not! Santa Lucia is completely different.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think you know better than me? I'm Sicilian! I know my own songs!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Dad, just because you're Sicilian doesn't mean you know every Italian song!

jokes.text: There's nothing floating in my drink.
jokes.setup: Tony saying he likes Singapore bar
jokes.punchline: His very low standards for bar quality
jokes.explanation: Excellent low expectations comedy. Implies terrible conditions at previous bars. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I like Singapore bar.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, any place where they don't check if you're on parole, that's a good bar.

jokes.text: But I tell you, I never thought I could make 'hell of a sailor.'
jokes.setup: Angie calling Tony a 'hell of a sailor'
jokes.punchline: Tony treating it as an achievement level to attain
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay treating 'hell of a sailor' as a specific rank or certification. Perfect Tony logic.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: You're a hell of a sailor, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think so? Maybe I should go for 'excellent sailor.' That's the next level up.

jokes.text: I ain't good enough to drink with you? / I suppose nobody here's good enough to drink with him.
jokes.setup: Drunk man approaching Tony and Angie
jokes.punchline: Escalating the confrontation to include everyone
jokes.explanation: Standard bar confrontation setup. Sets up the fight but isn't particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Drunk
dialogue.line: You looking at me? You looking at me?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, I'm not looking at you.

dialogue.speaker: Drunk
dialogue.line: Well, somebody's looking at me!

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: We're all looking at you now.

dialogue.speaker: Drunk
dialogue.line: That's right! All of you! Every one of you!

jokes.text: I didn't graduate. / Oh, good.
jokes.setup: Angie feeling guilty about missing Tony's graduation
jokes.punchline: Angie's relief that there was nothing to miss
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy - father's guilt instantly relieved by son's failure. Great character moment for both.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Tony, I feel terrible about missing your graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: But I wanted to be there for you.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: There's nothing to worry about. There was no graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: What do you mean there was no graduation?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I didn't graduate.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God!

jokes.text: This is better than Brooklyn.
jokes.setup: Tony and Angie in the middle of a bar fight
jokes.punchline: Tony preferring violent chaos to his hometown
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Tony finding violence preferable to Brooklyn says a lot about both. Excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what? I'll take this bar fight any day over going back to Brooklyn.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: At least here nobody knows my mother.

jokes.text: You know how I know when I've had too much to drink? / When's that? / I always wind up in a tattoo parlor.
jokes.setup: Tony asking about his drinking indicator
jokes.punchline: His specific pattern of ending up getting tattoos
jokes.explanation: Good personal logic joke with specific pattern recognition. Sets up the current situation perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Every time I drink, I wake up with a new tattoo. I've got so many now, I look like a biker bar threw up on me.

jokes.text: So tonight, I want you to cut me off before I... uh-oh. We're in a tattoo parlor.
jokes.setup: Tony asking to be cut off before getting a tattoo
jokes.punchline: Realizing he's already in a tattoo parlor
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback with great timing. The 'uh-oh' moment is perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Cut me off before I do something stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Tattoo Artist
dialogue.line: You mean like getting a tattoo?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, exactly. Just don't let me—

dialogue.speaker: Tattoo Artist
dialogue.line: You're already in the chair, pal.

jokes.text: Go live in a cave?
jokes.setup: Angie suggesting Tony get 'Tony' tattooed on his chest
jokes.punchline: Tony's logical concern about the implications
jokes.explanation: Good logical escalation of why having your own name tattooed would be problematic. Specific and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What if I fall in love with someone else? I'm gonna have to get her name tattooed right over it. By the time I'm thirty, my chest is gonna look like a roadmap of my life.

jokes.text: If we sober up, we won't do it.
jokes.setup: Angie asking if they should sober up before getting tattoos
jokes.punchline: Tony's practical understanding of drunk decision-making
jokes.explanation: Perfect drunk logic that's both stupid and wise. Great understanding of alcohol's role in bad decisions.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Should we sober up before we get tattoos?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, no. If we sober up, we'll realize what a stupid idea this is.

tmdb_overview: Tony's dad, a merchant seaman who Tony hasn't seen much of, arranges to bring Tony on a sea trip with him. Tony is hesitant but doesn't want to disappoint his dad, so he goes along. On the trip, Tony spends much of the time seasick, but bonds with his dad and they engage in a bar brawl in Singapore, and almost get matching tattoos until they think better of it.
insight: Deadpan taxi-driver dynamics sustain a 72-point episode across 47 jokes in tight quarters.
